content.text: Graduating an AI feature from beta to general availability (GA) is categorically different from promoting a conventional software release. Non-determinism, latency variance, and hallucination rates mean the old binary pass/fail checklist is insufficient. A structured
combines quantitative thresholds, qualitative readiness checks, and a standing decision forum with clear veto rights — ensuring that every GA release carries a defensible, documented decision trail.
What Beta Graduation Means for AI Features

For traditional software, beta graduation typically means: the code compiles, the happy path passes QA, and documentation is complete. For AI features, the challenge is that even a well-tested model behaves probabilistically. The same input can produce different outputs across runs; latency spikes when the upstream model provider has load; and edge-case prompts that weren't in the test set produce confusing or wrong answers in production.
Beta graduation for AI means you have sufficient

— not just pre-launch tests — that the feature behaves acceptably across real users, real queries, and real load. You are making a claim that the feature is ready for unrestricted access, and the graduation decision forum is the organizational mechanism that validates that claim.
a feature that hallucinates at 5% in beta will hallucinat at 5% in GA — but now with 10× the user base generating support tickets and social media complaints.
unlike a crashed server, a model that starts producing lower-quality answers doesn't throw an exception. Without explicit graduation criteria and post-GA monitoring, degradation goes undetected.
once users depend on a GA feature, rolling back the model version or prompt changes requires a deprecation process. The gate review prevents graduating features that lack a tested rollback path.
Support readiness is genuinely different for AI:
support teams need to know that the AI can refuse certain queries, that outputs are non-deterministic, and how to classify AI-related tickets — not just how to file a bug report.
What Makes AI Beta Graduation Unique
the same user query may produce subtly different outputs on different runs. Graduation criteria must account for output variance, not just correctness on a fixed eval set.
LLM call latency has a heavy tail. p50 latency might be 400ms, but p95 could be 3000ms if the model is under load. Both must be measured and thresholded separately.
a metric unique to generative AI. Requires a human-rated or LLM-judged sample of production outputs — not derivable from logs alone.
the fraction of user queries the feature can answer vs. routes to a fallback. Low coverage means users encounter unhelpful responses frequently even if the responses that are given are accurate.
teams that skipped building an offline eval suite during beta have no baseline to compare against post-launch. Graduation criteria should require that an eval suite exists and passes.

attrs.alt: Beta graduation gate review: Quantitative criteria → Qualitative criteria → Decision Forum → GA Release

content.text: Quantitative thresholds must be measured over a sustained window (30 days minimum) on real production traffic, not synthetic test data. Set hard and soft boundaries: a
requires a documented exception with a remediation timeline.

# graduation-criteria.yaml — hard and soft thresholds
# Measured over a rolling 30-day window on production beta traffic

quantitative:
  hard_blockers:                    # Non-waivable — blocks GA unconditionally
    error_rate_pct: 0.5             # % of requests returning 5xx or tool failure
    p95_latency_ms: 3000            # 95th-percentile end-to-end response time
    hallucination_rate_pct: 3.0     # Human-rated or LLM-judged sample (n ≥ 200)
    uptime_pct_30d: 99.0            # Availability of the AI feature endpoint

  soft_blockers:                    # Waivable with documented exception + owner
    p50_latency_ms: 800
    support_ticket_rate_per_100: 3  # Tickets attributable to AI errors per 100 MAU
    csat_score: 4.0                 # User-rated satisfaction (1–5 scale)
    coverage_pct: 85                # % of queries answered vs. routed to fallback
    eval_suite_pass_rate_pct: 95    # Offline eval suite pass rate (min 500 examples)

  measurement:
    window_days: 30
    min_traffic_samples: 5000       # Minimum requests needed for statistical validity
    hallucination_sample_size: 200  # Random sample rated by evaluators
Qualitative criteria cannot be measured by a dashboard — they require explicit sign-offs from named owners. Each item below maps to a
who attests completion before the gate review meeting.
Support team trained on AI-specific scenarios
Legal / compliance sign-off (data residency, PII handling)
SLO definitions approved and alerts configured
Monitoring dashboard published and linked in Confluence

The decision forum is a standing meeting — scheduled within 48 hours of the team declaring the checklist complete — not an ad-hoc Slack discussion. Formality matters: the outcome is recorded, signed off, and stored in the feature's audit trail.
Engineering Lead for the feature. Responsible for presenting the metrics dashboard and the completed checklist.
confirms user experience, pricing tier, and marketing readiness. Can raise soft blockers but does not hold a veto over technical criteria.
holds veto power over hard reliability blockers. If p95 latency or error rate thresholds are not met, the SRE representative blocks graduation regardless of PM pressure.
attests that the support team has been trained and that the support playbook covers AI-specific failure modes (hallucination, refusal, latency).
typically provides sign-off asynchronously before the forum. If sign-off is pending, the forum automatically defers.

## Beta Graduation Gate Review Checklist
**Feature:** AI Document Summarizer v2  
**Review Date:** 2025-03-15  
**Forum Chair:** @eng-lead  

### Quantitative (30-day window, n=12,450 requests)
- [x] Error rate: 0.3% ✅ (threshold: < 0.5%)
- [x] p95 latency: 1,840ms ✅ (threshold: < 3,000ms)
- [x] p50 latency: 520ms ✅ (threshold: < 800ms)
- [x] Hallucination rate: 1.8% ✅ (rated on 250-sample random draw)
- [x] Uptime: 99.7% ✅ (threshold: > 99.0%)
- [ ] Coverage: 82% ⚠️ SOFT BLOCKER — waiver approved, owner: @pm-lead
       Remediation: intent classifier improvement in Sprint 24
- [x] Support ticket rate: 1.9/100 MAU ✅
- [x] CSAT: 4.2/5.0 ✅
- [x] Eval suite pass rate: 97.3% (510 examples) ✅

### Qualitative
- [x] Support team trained: @support-lead attested 2025-03-10
- [x] Docs reviewed: @tech-writer merged on 2025-03-12
- [x] Rollback tested: @eng-lead ran drill on staging 2025-03-08
- [x] Runbook published: /runbooks/ai-doc-summarizer
- [x] Legal sign-off: @legal-counsel 2025-03-09
- [ ] SLO alerts: configured but PagerDuty routing not yet tested ⚠️
       Owner: @sre-oncall — must resolve before GA commit is merged

### Decision
**Outcome:** CONDITIONAL APPROVE  
**Conditions:** SLO alert routing verified by 2025-03-17  
**Signed:** @eng-lead, @pm-lead, @sre-oncall, @support-lead
The most common mistake in graduation reviews is treating all criteria as negotiable. Hard blockers must be enforced strictly — their value comes entirely from being non-waivable.
Error rate > threshold, rollback untested, legal sign-off missing
Coverage < threshold, CSAT borderline, dashboard not yet published
Tracking Graduation Metrics in a Dashboard
Build a graduation readiness dashboard that the team can check daily during the 30-day beta window — not a report assembled the night before the review meeting.

# graduation_dashboard.py — query Datadog/Grafana metrics and print readiness summary
import requests
import json
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

THRESHOLDS = {
    "error_rate_pct": {"hard": 0.5, "direction": "below"},
    "p95_latency_ms": {"hard": 3000, "direction": "below"},
    "hallucination_rate_pct": {"hard": 3.0, "direction": "below"},
    "uptime_pct": {"hard": 99.0, "direction": "above"},
    "csat_score": {"soft": 4.0, "direction": "above"},
    "coverage_pct": {"soft": 85.0, "direction": "above"},
    "support_tickets_per_100": {"soft": 3.0, "direction": "below"},
}

def check_metric(name: str, value: float) -> dict:
    rule = THRESHOLDS[name]
    direction = rule["direction"]
    blocker_type = "hard" if "hard" in rule else "soft"
    threshold = rule.get("hard") or rule.get("soft")
    if direction == "below":
        passed = value < threshold
    else:
        passed = value > threshold
    return {
        "metric": name,
        "value": value,
        "threshold": threshold,
        "blocker_type": blocker_type,
        "passed": passed,
        "status": "✅" if passed else ("🔴 HARD BLOCK" if blocker_type == "hard" else "⚠️ SOFT BLOCK"),
    }

def print_readiness_report(metrics: dict):
    print(f"=== Beta Graduation Readiness — {datetime.now().date()} ===")
    hard_blocked = False
    for name, value in metrics.items():
        result = check_metric(name, value)
        print(f"  {result['status']:20s} {name}: {value} (threshold: {result['threshold']})")
        if not result["passed"] and result["blocker_type"] == "hard":
            hard_blocked = True
    print()
    if hard_blocked:
        print("VERDICT: BLOCKED — resolve hard blockers before scheduling forum")
    else:
        print("VERDICT: READY FOR FORUM REVIEW")

# Example metrics (in production, fetch these from your observability stack)
print_readiness_report({
    "error_rate_pct": 0.3,
    "p95_latency_ms": 1840,
    "hallucination_rate_pct": 1.8,
    "uptime_pct": 99.7,
    "csat_score": 4.2,
    "coverage_pct": 82.0,  # soft blocker — waiver needed
    "support_tickets_per_100": 1.9,
})
Any AI feature touching user-facing outputs — copilots, summarizers, classifiers, search re-rankers — that has been in beta with a subset of users.
Major model version upgrades (e.g., upgrading to a newer Claude model) that change the output distribution, even if the feature UI does not change.
Agentic features with write-access tool use — where hallucination or incorrect tool calls produce side effects (emails sent, records modified).

load test results do not predict production latency distribution. Hallucination rates on synthetic prompts do not predict real user hallucination rates. All graduation metrics must be measured on production beta traffic.
a 7-day window misses weekly traffic patterns. LLM providers have weekend vs. weekday latency differences. Use at least 30 days.
if business pressure turns a "hard" blocker into a "soft" one mid-review, the framework loses its credibility. Keep hard blockers genuinely non-negotiable.
teams that skip the hallucination rate measurement because it is expensive to collect are making a blind bet. At minimum, run a 200-sample human-rated spot check.
a forum where every participant has equal say means the PM can outvote the SRE on a reliability blocker. Veto rights must be explicitly assigned before the first meeting.
Automate the dashboard so the team sees readiness status daily — not just in the gate review meeting.
Run the rollback drill at least two weeks before the planned graduation date — not the day before.
Write the support playbook before opening the beta — the beta period is where you discover the AI failure modes that need to be documented.
Set thresholds during roadmap planning, not during the graduation review. Thresholds set during the review get anchored to actual observed metrics and become self-fulfilling.
After GA, re-run the same metric checks at 30 and 90 days to confirm no regression from broader traffic distribution.
GA traffic may be 10–50× beta traffic. Validate that the model provider rate limits and the token budget support the projected GA volume before graduation.
p95 latency at GA load may differ from beta. If possible, run a load test with GA-level traffic in staging before the forum.
Include cost per request in the graduation criteria — features that are economically unviable at scale should be flagged before GA even if reliability criteria pass.
Datadog, Grafana, or CloudWatch for error rate, latency, and uptime. Custom span attributes required for AI-specific metrics (model used, token count, evaluation score).
Labelbox, Scale AI, or an LLM-judge pipeline using a stronger model to rate a random sample of production outputs.
Linear or Jira with a "Beta Graduation" issue template. GitHub Projects works for teams already living in GitHub.
Confluence page per feature with the completed checklist, the forum decision, and any waiver documentation — stored as the permanent audit trail.
Explicit accountability, audit trail, hard blockers enforced
Requires 30-day measurement window, forum scheduling overhead
No engineering/SRE veto; reliability blockers get overridden by business pressure
No moment where qualitative criteria (docs, training) are formally checked
Misses support training, docs, and legal readiness — common post-launch failures
Require a prompt injection test pass as a hard blocker for agentic features before GA. A feature that can be trivially jailbroken in beta should not graduate.
Verify that PII logging is off by default before GA. Beta often runs with verbose logging for debugging — those settings must be reverted before unrestricted access.
Confirm that the rate-limiting policy for the AI feature is in place at GA — without it, a single abusive user can exhaust model provider quotas for all users.
Think of the graduation gate as a

for aircraft. The checklist does not make the flight safer on its own — the work done before the checklist does. But the checklist is the mechanism that ensures the work was actually done, by the right person, before takeoff. The decision forum is the co-pilot who reads each item aloud and gets a verbal confirmation. The hard blockers are the items where a missing checkmark means the plane does not leave the gate.
AI beta graduation requires AI-specific quantitative criteria: error rate, p95 latency, hallucination rate, and coverage — all measured on production traffic over at least 30 days.
Hard blockers are non-waivable. Soft blockers can be waived with a documented plan and named owner. Treat this distinction seriously or the framework loses its value.
Qualitative criteria — support training, documentation, rollback testing, legal sign-off — are as important as quantitative ones and often the ones that get skipped under launch pressure.
The decision forum needs explicit veto rights: SRE vetos on reliability, Legal vetos on compliance. Without veto structure, business pressure overrides technical blockers.
Automate the graduation dashboard — teams that build a daily readiness view graduate features faster and with fewer surprises than teams that assemble a report the week of the review.
After GA, run the same metric checks at 30 and 90 days to catch post-GA regressions caused by the broader traffic distribution that beta traffic did not capture.
